In India, interest in sociological... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY, CHAPTER 5, INDIAN SOCIOLOGISTS, 8 3 INDIAN SOCIOLOGISTS. full_notes:
Indian Sociologists is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY CHAPTER 5 INDIAN SOCIOLOGISTS As you saw in the opening chapter of your first book, Introducing Sociology, the discipline is a relatively young one even in the European context, having been established only about a century ago.

The text explains that in India, interest in sociological ways of thinking is a little more than a century old, but formal university teaching of sociology only began in 1919 at the University of Bombay.

The text explains that in the 1920s, two other universities — those at Calcutta and Lucknow — also began programmes of teaching and research in sociology and anthropology.

The text explains that today, every major university has a department of sociology, social anthropology or anthropology, and often more than one of these disciplines is represented.

The text explains that now-a-days sociology tends to be taken for granted in India, like most established things.

The text explains that in the early days, it was not clear at all what an Indian sociology would look like, and indeed, whether India really needed something like sociology.

The text explains that in the first quarter of the 20th century, those who became interested in the discipline had to decide for themselves what role it could play in India.

The text explains that in this chapter, you are going to be introduced to some of the founding figures of Indian sociology.

The text explains that these scholars have helped to shape the discipline and adapt it to our historical and social context.

The text explains that the specificity of the Indian context raised many questions.

The text explains that first of all, if western sociology emerged as an attempt to make sense of modernity, what would its role be in a country like India?

The text explains that india, too, was of course experiencing the changes brought about by moder nity but with an important difference — it was a colony.

The text explains that the first experience of modernity in India was closely intertwined with the experience of colonial subjugation.

The text explains that secondly, if social anthropology in the west arose out of the curiosity felt by European society about primitive cultures, what role could it have in India, which was an ancient and advanced civilisation, but which also had ‘primitive’ societies within it?

The text explains that finally, what useful role could sociology have in a sovereign, independent India, a nation about to begin its adventure with planned development and democracy?

The text explains that 8 3 INDIAN SOCIOLOGISTS The pioneers of Indian sociology not only had to find their own answers to questions like these, they also had to for mulate new questions for themselves.

The text explains that it was only through the experience of ‘doing’ sociology in an Indian context that the questions took shape — they were not available ‘readymade’.

The text explains that the British government wanted similar surveys done in all the princely states as well as the presidency areas directly under its control.

The text explains that this decision was to change his life, for he remained in Ranchi for the next forty- four years and became the leading authority on the culture and society of the tribal peoples of the Chhotanagpur region (present day Jharkhand).

The text explains that roy became deeply interested in tribal society as a byproduct of his

The text explains that 84 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY professional need to interpret tribal customs and laws to the court.

The text explains that the four Indian sociologists you are going to be introduced in this chapter were born one generation later than Iyer and Roy.

The text explains that although they were all deeply influenced by western traditions of sociology, they were also able to offer some initial answers to the question that the pioneers could only begin to ask : what shape should a specifically Indian sociology take?

The text explains that he also founded the Indian Sociological Society as well as its jour nal Sociological Bulletin.

The text explains that ghurye’s Bombay University department was the first to successfully implement two of the features which were later enthusiastically endorsed by his successors in the discipline.

The text explains that 1951: Ghurye established the Indian Sociological Society and became its founding President.

The text explains that the journal of the Indian Sociological Society, Sociological Bulletin was launched in 1952.

The text explains that 86 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY specially his debate with Verrier Elwin which first made him known outside sociology and the academic world.

The text explains that they also believed that the innocent and simple tribals would suffer exploitation and cultural degradation through contact with Hindu culture and society.

The text explains that for this reason, they felt that the state had a duty to protect the tribes and to help them sustain their way of life and culture, which were facing constant pressure to assimilate with mainstream Hindu culture.

The text explains that however, nationalist Indians were equally passionate about their belief in the unity of India and the need for moder nising Indian society and culture.

The text explains that as with many features of Hinduism itself which they felt to be backward and in need of reform, they felt that tribes, too, needed to develop.

The text explains that they were thus simply further behind in the same process of assimilation that all Indian communities had gone through.

The text explains that ghurye and the nationalists, on the other hand, argued that these ill-effects were not specific to tribal cultures, but were common to all the backward and downtrodden sections of Indian society.

The text explains that discuss the different sides to the question from a contemporary perspective.

The text explains that in other parts of India, the inter -group differences in the anthropometric measurements were not very large or systematic.

The text explains that 88 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY have been introduced into groups that were already racially varied.

The text explains that (iv) Following from the principles of hierarchy and restricted social interaction, caste also involves differential rights and duties for different castes.

The text explains that these rights and duties pertain not only to religious practices but extend to the secular world.

The text explains that as ethnographic accounts of everyday life in caste society have shown, interactions between people of different castes are governed by these rules.

The text explains that at the level of society, caste functions as a rigid form of the division of labour with specific occupations being allocated to specific castes.

The text explains that undergraduate degree in science and postgraduate degrees in History and Economics from Calcutta University.

The text explains that 1924: Appointed Lecturer in the Department of Economics and Sociology at Lucknow University 1938-41 Served as Director of Information under the first Congress-led government of the United Provinces of British India (present day Uttar Pradesh).

The text explains that 1953: Appointed Professor of Economics at Aligarh Muslim University 1955: Presidential Address to the newly formed Indian Sociological Society 1956: Underwent major surgery for throat cancer in Switzerland Died on 5 December 1961. of rules about eligible and non- eligible groups helps reproduce the caste system.

The text explains that ghurye’s definition helped to make the study of caste more systematic.

The text explains that in actual practice, many of these features of caste were changing, though all of them continue to exist in some form.

The text explains that 90 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY his scholarly writings as from his teaching, his speaking at academic events, and his work in the media, including newspaper articles and radio programmes.

The text explains that indian Sociology: Reflections and Introspections, Popular Prakashan, Bombay.

The text explains that pathways: Approaches to the Study of Society in India.

The text explains that pATEL, SUJATA. ‘Towards a Praxiological Understanding of Indian Society: The Sociology of A.R.

The text explains that desai’, in Uberoi, Sundar and Deshpande (eds) (in press).

The text explains that development Department, Government of West Bengal.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society, Chapter 5: Indian Sociologists

schemes.name: The British
schemes.point: The British government wanted similar surveys done in all the princely states as well as the presidency areas directly under its control. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 1)

schemes.name: These
schemes.point: These were the active combining of teaching and research within the same institution, and the merger of social anthropology and sociology into a composite discipline. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 2)

schemes.name: Programme
schemes.point: Programme was launched at the Bombay Department; the first Ph.D. in Sociology at an Indian university was awarded to G.R. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 3)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The M.A. course was revised and made a full-fledged 8-course programme in 1945. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 3)

schemes.name: Caste
schemes.point: (i) Caste is an institution based on segmental division. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch5 pg 6)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY and CHAPTER 5 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Indian Sociologists; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 82 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY and CHAPTER 5 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
